subject: Biology
vision: Quality, life long education for all which is accessible, inclusive and relevant to individual, national and global needs

sub_topics.title: Nature of Science - Inquiry in Biology
competences: Apply scientific inquiry in carrying out scientific investigations

instructional_blocks.hook: How do scientists investigate biological phenomena?
teacher_steps: Describe the scientific inquiry method
Guide learners through the stages of scientific inquiry
Demonstrate how to observe, identify problems, and form hypotheses
Show examples of testing hypotheses through experimentation
Facilitate data recording and analysis activities
Help learners draw conclusions from investigations
learner_steps: Investigating the stages of scientific inquiry (observation, identifying a problem, forming hypothesis, testing hypothesis through experimentation, recording and analysing data, forming a conclusion)
Applying scientific inquiry method in investigating biological facts and phenomena given a situation
Sharing the findings of the investigations with peers
Using the findings of the investigations to solve real life problems
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Scientific inquiry is a systematic approach to investigating biological phenomena. It involves making observations, asking questions, forming testable hypotheses, conducting experiments, collecting and analyzing data, and drawing evidence-based conclusions. This method forms the foundation of all biological research and helps learners develop critical thinking, problem-solving skills, and scientific literacy. The process is iterative and may require multiple rounds of investigation to reach reliable conclusions.

sub_topics.short_notes: Scientific inquiry is the foundation of biological investigation. It provides a structured, evidence-based approach to understanding living organisms and their interactions with the environment. Through scientific inquiry, biologists can test ideas, answer questions, and expand our understanding of life.

vocabulary.definition: A systematic approach to investigating phenomena through observation, hypothesis formation, experimentation, data analysis, and conclusion drawing
The act of carefully watching and noting phenomena as they occur in nature or in a laboratory setting
A testable prediction or proposed explanation for an observation based on prior knowledge
A controlled test designed to investigate a hypothesis by manipulating variables
A factor that can be changed, controlled, or measured in an experiment
Information collected during an experiment or observation, which can be qualitative or quantitative
A judgment or decision reached by reasoning from evidence gathered during investigation

questions: Design a simple scientific investigation to test a biological hypothesis
Identify and explain each step of scientific inquiry in a given scenario
Explain why scientific inquiry is important in biology
Apply the scientific method to investigate a real-life biological problem in your environment
Distinguish between a hypothesis and a conclusion
Describe how scientific inquiry helps us understand living things

sub_topics.title: Nature of Biology

instructional_blocks.hook: What are the different areas of study in Biology and why do we need them?
teacher_steps: Introduce the nature of Biology as the study of life
Explain the different branches of Biology and their focus areas
Provide examples of what each branch studies
Guide learners to explore each branch through examples
Help learners create a concept map showing relationships between branches
Discuss how branches of Biology interconnect and support each other
learner_steps: Identifying the branches of biology (botany, zoology, entomology, genetics, mycology)
Creating a concept map on the nature of Biology
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Biology is the scientific study of life and living organisms. It is divided into several specialized branches to facilitate in-depth study of specific groups or aspects of life. Major branches include: Botany (study of plants), Zoology (study of animals), Entomology (study of insects), Genetics (study of heredity and variation), and Mycology (study of fungi). Each branch uses scientific methods to understand its particular focus area, contributing to our comprehensive understanding of life on Earth.

sub_topics.short_notes: Biology encompasses various specialized branches, each focusing on different aspects of life. These branches work together to provide a comprehensive understanding of living organisms, their structures, functions, interactions, and evolution.

vocabulary.definition: The scientific study of life and living organisms
The branch of biology that deals with the scientific study of plants
The branch of biology concerned with the study of animals
The scientific study of insects
The study of heredity and variation in organisms
The branch of biology concerned with the study of fungi

questions: List and describe the main branches of Biology
Give three examples of what each branch of Biology studies
Create a concept map showing the relationships between different branches of Biology
Explain why Biology is divided into different specialized branches
Match biological topics to their appropriate branches
Describe how different branches of Biology might work together to solve a problem

sub_topics.title: Levels of Biological Organisation
competences: Classify the levels of biological organisation from simple to complex

instructional_blocks.hook: How is life organized from the smallest unit to the largest system?
teacher_steps: Introduce the concept of hierarchical biological organization
Use diagrams, charts, and models to illustrate each level
Guide learners to arrange levels from simple to complex
Provide clear examples at each level of organization
Explain how each level builds upon the previous one
Discuss the importance of organization in living systems
learner_steps: Classifying levels of biological organisation (atom, molecule, cell, tissue, organ, system, organism, population, communities, ecosystem, biosphere)
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Living organisms are organized in a hierarchical manner from the smallest units to the largest systems. The levels are: Atoms (smallest chemical units) → Molecules (atoms bonded together) → Cells (basic units of life) → Tissues (groups of similar cells) → Organs (tissues working together) → Organ Systems (organs working together) → Organisms (individual living things) → Populations (groups of same species) → Communities (different populations together) → Ecosystems (communities plus environment) → Biosphere (all ecosystems on Earth). Each level exhibits properties that emerge from the organization of components at the level below it.

sub_topics.short_notes: Biological organization represents the hierarchy of complex biological structures and systems. Understanding these levels helps learners appreciate how life is structured from simple atoms to complex ecosystems, and how each level contributes to the functioning of the whole.

vocabulary.definition: The smallest unit of a chemical element
Two or more atoms chemically bonded together
The basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms
A group of similar cells working together to perform a specific function
A structure composed of different tissues working together to perform specific functions
A group of organs working together to perform complex body functions
An individual living thing that can carry out all life processes
A group of organisms of the same species living in the same area at the same time
All the different populations living and interacting in a particular area
A community of living organisms interacting with their physical environment
The global sum of all ecosystems; the zone of life on Earth

questions: Arrange the levels of biological organisation from simplest to most complex
Give specific examples of each level of biological organisation
Explain how each level of organisation builds upon the previous one
Identify the level of biological organisation in given examples
Describe the relationship between cells, tissues, organs, and organ systems
Compare populations, communities, and ecosystems

sub_topics.title: Characteristics of Living Things
competences: Analyse the characteristics of living things
Categorise life processes of living organisms

instructional_blocks.hook: What makes something alive? How can we tell living things from non-living things?
teacher_steps: Introduce the seven characteristics of living things
Demonstrate each characteristic using real organisms or examples
Guide learners to observe living things exhibiting these characteristics
Help learners distinguish living from non-living things
Explain life processes including metabolism (catabolism and anabolism)
Facilitate discussions on why all characteristics must be present
learner_steps: Investigating the characteristics of living things (feeding/nutrition, respiration, excretion, reproduction, sensitivity/irritability, movement/locomotion, growth)
Distinguishing living things from non-living things
Describing life processes in living organisms
Analysing life processes in living organisms
Categorising life processes of living organisms (catabolism and anabolism)
instructional_blocks.short_notes: All living organisms share seven key characteristics that distinguish them from non-living things: 1) Nutrition - obtaining and using food for energy and growth; 2) Respiration - releasing energy from food; 3) Excretion - removing waste products from metabolism; 4) Reproduction - producing offspring; 5) Sensitivity/Irritability - detecting and responding to environmental changes; 6) Movement - changing position or place; 7) Growth - permanent increase in size and complexity. Life processes are categorized into catabolism (breaking down complex molecules to release energy) and anabolism (building complex molecules using energy). Together, these processes constitute metabolism.

sub_topics.short_notes: Living organisms exhibit specific characteristics that distinguish them from non-living matter. These characteristics work together to maintain life and ensure species survival. Understanding these characteristics is fundamental to studying biology.

vocabulary.definition: The process by which organisms obtain and use food for energy, growth, and repair
The biochemical process of releasing energy from food molecules
The removal of metabolic waste products from the body
The biological process by which organisms produce offspring
The ability to detect and respond to changes in the internal or external environment
Change in position or place, either of the whole organism or parts of it
A permanent increase in size, mass, and complexity of an organism
All chemical reactions occurring in living organisms
The breakdown of complex molecules into simpler ones, releasing energy
The building up of complex molecules from simpler ones, requiring energy

questions: List and explain the seven characteristics of living things
Distinguish between living and non-living things using specific examples
Explain the difference between catabolism and anabolism with examples
Observe an organism and describe how it exhibits each characteristic of life
Why must all seven characteristics be present for something to be considered alive?
Give examples of catabolic and anabolic processes in the human body

sub_topics.title: Microscopes
competences: Use different types of microscopes to examine specimens

instructional_blocks.hook: How can we see things that are too small for the naked eye to see?
teacher_steps: Introduce different types of microscopes (simple and compound)
Demonstrate the parts of a compound microscope and their functions
Show proper techniques for preparing specimen slides
Demonstrate mounting procedures
Guide learners through proper focusing techniques
Teach how to calculate magnification
Emphasize care and maintenance of microscopes
learner_steps: Exploring different types of microscopes (simple and compound microscopes)
Relating parts of a compound microscope to the functions (eye piece lens, objective lenses, body tube, adjustment knobs revolving nose piece, stage, diaphragm, aperture, light source, arm and base)
Practicing the use of microscopes (preparing the specimen slides, mounting, focussing and observing)
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Microscopes are essential scientific tools that allow biologists to observe structures invisible to the naked eye. There are two main types: simple microscopes (magnifying glasses with one lens) and compound microscopes (use multiple lenses for higher magnification). A compound microscope has several key parts: eyepiece lens (typically 10x magnification), objective lenses (various magnifications: 4x, 10x, 40x), stage (holds the slide), focusing knobs (coarse and fine adjustment), diaphragm (controls light), and light source. Total magnification is calculated by multiplying eyepiece magnification by objective lens magnification (e.g., 10x × 40x = 400x total magnification). Proper care includes keeping lenses clean, carrying with both hands, and storing in a safe place.

sub_topics.short_notes: Microscopes revolutionized biology by revealing the microscopic world. Proper understanding and use of microscopes is essential for studying cells, tissues, and microorganisms. These instruments remain fundamental tools in biological research and education.

vocabulary.definition: An optical instrument that uses lenses to magnify small objects or organisms
A microscope with a single lens, like a magnifying glass
A microscope with multiple lenses that provides higher magnification
The degree to which an object's image is enlarged by an optical instrument
The ability to distinguish two close points as separate
A sample of material prepared for examination under a microscope
A thin flat piece of glass on which specimens are mounted for microscopic examination
The lens closest to the eye in a microscope
The lens closest to the specimen in a microscope
The platform on a microscope where the slide is placed
The part that controls the amount of light passing through the specimen

questions: Label the parts of a compound microscope
Explain the function of each major part of the microscope
Calculate total magnification: If the eyepiece is 10x and the objective lens is 40x, what is the total magnification?
Demonstrate proper technique for preparing and observing a specimen slide
Compare and contrast simple and compound microscopes
Describe the proper care and maintenance of a microscope
Explain why microscopes are important tools in biology

descriptors: Compile data, create mental images and address issues
Use mathematical/scientific language in different situations
Express oneself using different media and symbols
Play with peers to build relationships
Participate in and express themselves through play activities
Classify objects according to their attributes
Dispose trash in the designated place
Adhere to best practices in environmental management
Identify types of waste in local environment
Make connections/link with the inner world or social environment
Use numeracy patterns and relations to solve problems
Manipulate numbers, shapes and symbols to complete a task

continuous_assessment.description: Conducted in line with concepts and topics using various testing methods
